blurb:
Two months old, two pounds, and absolutely convinced that every toy in the room is his. Malik came to Mostly Mutts from animal control alongside his friend Madelyn, went into a foster home right away, and has just moved over to the shelter's Kitty Aquarium. He's ready to find his people.

His foster described him as curious and super playful, the kind of kitten who throws himself into whatever is happening and figures out the details later. He's litter trained, does well with other cats, and spent time in a foster home with a calm dog — he was getting used to them gradually, so a laid-back dog at home isn't out of the question. He does well with gentle kids too.

Madelyn is also still available, so if you've ever considered adopting two at once — and honestly, two kittens are usually easier than one — these two already know each other. Either way, Malik is a good one. He's at Mostly Mutts in Kennesaw. Swing by the Kitty Aquarium to meet him or reach out through their website to get started.

Mostly Mutts Animal Rescue
2769 George Busbee Pkwy NW, Kennesaw, GA 30144
(770) 975-0004 | info@mostlymutts.org
shelter_address: 2769 George Busbee Pkwy NW, Kennesaw, GA 30144
scoring_notes:
• Classic high-appeal kitten profile — playful, litter trained, dog-tolerant — nearly any household qualifies
• Bonded pair angle with Madelyn is a strong double-adoption pitch that drives newsletter engagement
• Timeline mirrors Madelyn's, suggesting a recent intake with no pressing urgency signal yet

blurb:
Jacquelyn came into rescue from animal control, landed in a foster home with three other dogs, and has been living her best life ever since. She's two years old, about 58 pounds, and her foster describes her as lovable, huggable, and genuinely fun to be around. She knows her commands, loves to play, and will absolutely wiggle her way into the center of your attention — not in a pushy way, just in a "this is my person and I intend to be near them" kind of way.

She does have a congenital kidney defect, which sounds scarier than it is. Her foster says it's easily managed — just a few regular potty breaks during the day, no more than any normal dog needs. She sleeps through the night without a single trip outside. She's living fully and happily, and her foster wants her family to know that going in. She'd do best with someone who works from home or has a flexible schedule, and she's great with other friendly dogs. No cats, and no toddlers she might accidentally bowl over with enthusiasm.

Mostly Mutts is a rescue based in Kennesaw. Visit their website to submit an inquiry and set up a meet with Jacquelyn.
shelter_hours: See website for current hours: mostlymutts.org
scoring_notes:
• The 'health condition that isn't scary' framing is a powerful editorial hook that challenges reader assumptions and builds empathy
• Foster's insistence on full transparency about the kidney defect adds credibility and human warmth that resonates in newsletter format
• Work-from-home requirement and cat/toddler restrictions narrow the pool meaningfully, suggesting she may have been waiting longer than average

blurb:
Her foster calls her the best cat ever, and honestly, that tracks. Gina is about 11 months old, only five pounds, and has basically maintained full kitten mode despite being nearly a year old. She came to Mostly Mutts as part of the Go-Gos litter, got adopted, and then came back after developing some stomach issues. Several months of vet care and food changes later, she's doing well — though she does still vomit sometimes and the cause hasn't been pinned down. That's the honest truth, and the right person for Gina already knows that some cats just have sensitive systems and it's not a dealbreaker.

Outside of that, she is genuinely sweet, playful, and independent enough to entertain herself but snuggly enough to want to curl up with you when the mood hits. She lives with two adult cats in her foster home and does great with them. She's also met a leashed dog without flinching, and she's been good around kids who know how to be gentle.

Because she's on a special diet, she'd do best as an only cat — or in a home where feeding time can be managed separately so she's not getting into another cat's food. If that sounds workable, she's worth meeting. Mostly Mutts is in Kennesaw. You can start the process on their website or visit during adoption hours.

• Return adoption plus months of vet care strongly implies extended time in the system — urgency is real

blurb:
Josie has the most ridiculous floppy ears and a nubby little tail, and once you see her in person that's probably going to be the end of your decision-making process. She's a six-year-old hound mix, about 35 pounds, and she came back to Mostly Mutts after a short adoption because her energy level wasn't a match for the family. That's not a knock on Josie — it's just useful information.

She went through the prison foster training program and graduated, so she's got some skills and structure behind her. Her foster rates her energy at a 7 or 8, but says her cuddle level matches it. She wants a long walk or a good play session, and then she wants to be on the couch with you. If that sounds like your weekend, she's going to fit right in.

She does best as an only dog and no cats — she's tried both and it wasn't her thing. She hasn't met many kids yet, but her foster thinks 10 and up could be a good match since she plays with her mouth a little, not aggressively, just enthusiastically. She's sweet and social with people once she's past the initial introduction, which doesn't take long.

Mostly Mutts handles adoptions through an application and meet-and-greet. Head to their website to get started, or stop by their location in Marietta.
scoring_notes:
• Prison training program graduation is a standout credential that signals structure and gives readers a feel-good hook
• Return adoption with a clear, non-alarming explanation makes her story relatable and easy for the editor to frame positively
• Six years old, returned once, and only-pet requirement together indicate a longer road to the right match

blurb:
Madelyn came into Mostly Mutts from animal control at about two and a half months old, barely two and a half pounds, and has been winning people over ever since. She and her buddy Malik went straight into a foster home, got properly socialized, and have now moved into the shelter's Kitty Aquarium — which is a great sign that she's ready and confident enough to meet the public.

She's a tortoiseshell with a lot of kitten energy and a curiosity level that her foster described as pretty relentless in the best way. She's litter trained, likes toys (all of them, apparently — no playing favorites), and loves being around both people and other cats. She warmed up gradually to a calm dog in her foster home, so a patient, cat-tolerant dog at home isn't automatically a no.

If you've been thinking about getting a kitten, Madelyn is the kind of cat who makes the decision easy. She's social, she's playful, and she's at the stage where everything is still new and worth investigating. Mostly Mutts is in Kennesaw. Stop by to meet her at the Kitty Aquarium or start an inquiry through their website.

blurb:
Madelyn came in from animal control at about 2.5 months old, went straight into a foster home, and has recently moved to the Kitty Aquarium at Mostly Mutts in Kennesaw. She weighs about 2.5 pounds right now, which means she is absolutely tiny, and she is apparently very aware of how fun that is.

Her foster described her as curious and super playful, the kind of kitten who treats every corner of a room like it needs investigating and every toy like it was invented specifically for her. She's been litter trained since the start, got along well with the cats she's lived with, and was slowly warming up to a calm dog in her foster home. She likes people, she likes other cats, and she seems pretty enthusiastic about life in general.

If you have a cat at home who's been flying solo for a while, Madelyn is the kind of addition that tends to make everyone's life more interesting. She's ready to meet you at Mostly Mutts on Kennesaw Due West Road. You can visit her in person at the Kitty Aquarium or start the process through their website.

blurb:
Josie has floppy ears, a nubby little tail, and more energy than her last family knew what to do with. She's six years old, 35 pounds, a hound mix, and she was returned not because anything was wrong with her — her energy level just wasn't the right fit. So now she's looking for someone who actually wants an adventure buddy. Think trail walks, active weekends, a person who doesn't mind a dog that keeps up.

Here's the flip side: her foster says her cuddle level matches her energy level. So after all that, she wants to be on the couch with you. She completed the Mostly Mutts prison training program, loves people, and even warmed up during her shelter bath enough to start giving kisses by the end of it. She does some mouthy, playful nibbling — never mean, just enthusiastic — so older kids around 10 and up would be a better fit than little ones.

She'd prefer to be your only dog, and cats are a no. If you've got the energy and a couch to share at the end of the day, go meet Josie at Mostly Mutts in Kennesaw. You can submit an inquiry through their website to get started.
shelter_hours: See website for current hours: mostlymutts.org
scoring_notes:
• Returned for being 'too much dog' is a deeply relatable and redemptive story that will resonate with active readers looking for a real companion
• Prison training program credential plus the bath-kissing detail gives editors two vivid, shareable moments in a single blurb
• A six-year-old returned dog with an only-dog, no-cat profile has likely been waiting significantly — high urgency for the right reader

blurb:
Yasmeen is eight years old, about 58 pounds, and she has spent enough time figuring out how the world works that she is done tolerating people she doesn't trust. That sounds like a warning, but it's actually the most honest thing you can say about a dog who, once she does trust you, becomes completely devoted. Her foster mom says adopters are going to "hit the jackpot" with her. That tracks.

She went through a prison foster and training program, which gave her structure and helped her build skills for meeting new people. The key for Yasmeen is doing introductions outside her space — not at the front door, not inside the house. Meet her on neutral ground, let her decide you're okay, and she will remember that. Her foster reports that once that trust is established, she's settled in well and is much more relaxed about new visitors.

She's good with cats, which is not nothing, and she liked the dogs she met at the rescue. She may come across as dominant at first with other dogs but shifts into play mode quickly. Kids 15 and up are the better fit — she did some guarding in her temporary environment, so younger kids would need a meetup with her trainer involved. If you want a calm, loyal, affectionate dog who takes her job as your companion seriously, Yasmeen is worth the trip.

Mostly Mutts handles adoptions through an application and in-person meeting. Visit their website to submit an inquiry and set up a time to meet her.
scoring_notes:
• Eight years old with a trust-first personality and a prison training backstory is the most layered and newsworthy profile in this batch
• Foster's 'jackpot' endorsement paired with honest caveats about introductions gives the editor a ready-made narrative arc
• Senior dog, selective trust, trainer-required kid meetup, and specific intro protocol all point to an extended and difficult-to-place wait

blurb:
Her foster family calls her the best cat ever, and they mean it. Gina is about 11 months old, barely 5 pounds, and fully committed to staying that way. She came in with a litter of siblings, got adopted, and then came back after some stomach issues started showing up. After months of vet care and food changes, she's doing well, though she does still vomit occasionally and the cause hasn't been pinned down. That's the honest version, and it's worth knowing going in.

What's also worth knowing: none of that has touched her personality. She's playful, affectionate, and apparently great with people, other cats, and even dogs she's met on a leash. Her foster says she's independent enough to do her own thing but absolutely loves to snuggle when she's in the mood. She has maintained full kitten energy at nearly a year old, which is exactly as delightful as it sounds.

Because she's on a special diet, a home where she's the only cat is easiest, but a separate feeding setup with another laid-back cat could absolutely work too. If you've been on the fence about adding a cat to your home, Gina is a pretty compelling reason to get off it. She's at Mostly Mutts in Kennesaw. Submit an adoption inquiry through their website to get started.
shelter_address: 3832 Kennesaw Due West Rd NW, Kennesaw, GA 30152
shelter_hours: Please check mostlymutts.org for current hours
scoring_notes:
• Rare transparency about health history paired with glowing foster praise creates rare reader trust and emotional investment
• Adopted-and-returned arc with a happy recovery ending is a compelling redemption narrative tailor-made for newsletter readers
• Months of vet care and a second stint in rescue signal meaningful time waiting — urgency to find her a permanent home is real

blurb:
Yasmeen is eight years old, came from animal control, went through a prison training program, landed in a foster home, and her foster mom says whoever adopts her is going to hit the jackpot. That's the kind of endorsement that means something.

She takes a little time to trust new people — that's just who she is. But the training program showed it doesn't take long. Once she knows you, she's loyal and loving and will protect her people like that's her whole job. She's good with cats and has gotten along with other dogs after a quick initial sizing-up period. She'd do best with dog-savvy kids 15 and up, and it's recommended that new people meet her outside the home first rather than walking straight into her space — a small adjustment that makes a big difference for her.

Yasmeen is a dog that needs the right fit, not a difficult one. If you're patient, consistent, and looking for a devoted companion who takes her loyalty seriously, she's worth going to meet. Reach out to Mostly Mutts in Kennesaw through their website to set up an introduction.
shelter_hours: See website for current hours: mostlymutts.org
scoring_notes:
• Eight years old, from animal control, through a prison program, into foster — the layered journey makes her the most narratively rich candidate in the group
• Foster's 'jackpot' endorsement combined with nuanced trust-building needs gives editors a story about earning loyalty that readers will remember
• Age, specific kid and intro requirements, and animal-control origins together strongly suggest an extended wait — this is the highest-urgency feature candidate

blurb:
Malik is 2.5 months old, about 2 pounds, and has apparently already decided that toys exist to be conquered and laps exist to be warmed. He came into Mostly Mutts from animal control alongside his friend Madelyn, spent time in a foster home, and recently moved to the Kitty Aquarium at the shelter. He is ready for the next chapter.

His foster described him as curious and super playful, which tracks for a kitten his age, but also just genuinely sweet. He's good with other cats, was slowly getting comfortable around a calm dog in his foster home, and keeps his space clean on his own. He likes people, he likes toys, and based on everything his foster said, he's pretty easy to be around.

At 2 pounds, he's going to need a little time to grow into himself, but his personality is already fully formed. If you've got room in your home and maybe another cat who'd enjoy some company, Malik is worth a visit. He's living at Mostly Mutts on Kennesaw Due West Road right now. Stop by to meet him or submit an inquiry through their website.
shelter_address: 3832 Kennesaw Due West Rd NW, Kennesaw, GA 30152

• Easygoing, playful, litter-trained kitten with strong social skills makes for one of the most adoptable candidates in the group

blurb:
Jacquelyn came into rescue from animal control, landed in a foster home, and by all accounts has been thriving ever since. She's about two years old, weighs around 58 pounds, and her foster describes her as lovable, huggable, and genuinely fun to have around. She's also a self-appointed house announcer — nothing gets past her — but once you say "OK Jacquelyn, I got it," she settles right down.

She's a Velcro dog through and through. She wants to be near you, involved in what you're doing, and first in line for any available affection. She's learned "sit," "no," and "back" in foster, so she's not pushy about it — just enthusiastic. She loves the three dogs in her foster home and would be thrilled to have a dog sibling in her new place.

One thing worth knowing: Jacquelyn has a congenital kidney defect. It's manageable and hasn't slowed her down at all — she goes out a normal number of times during the day and sleeps through the night without issue. She'd do best with someone who works from home or has a flexible schedule. She's not a medical project, she's just a dog who needs a little extra consideration and will give back everything she's got. No cats, and probably no toddlers given her enthusiasm level, but older kids are worth a meetup.

Mostly Mutts is a rescue-based organization in Kennesaw. Adoptions are handled through an application and meet-and-greet process. Visit their website to get started.
scoring_notes:
• Congenital kidney defect handled with total transparency makes her the newsletter's most emotionally resonant dog story
• Velcro dog personality with real obedience progress is a compelling combination that offsets the medical nuance
• Work-from-home requirement and medical condition narrow the adopter pool, suggesting she has been waiting longer than average
